rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Special Motion To Strike Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 4, CROSS DEFENDANT SHENG LUI's Special Motion To Strike Complaint. Non-party Sheng Liu's Anti-SLAPP Motion to Strike is denied. Sheng Liu filed this motion claiming that he was sued as Roe 1. The record does not show, however, that Bing Wu ever substituted Sheng Liu into this case as a cross-defendant. An anti-SLAPP motion may only be brought by a "party" against whom a cause of action has been asserted. (Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(b)(1), (j)(1); Foundation for Taxpayer & Consumer Rights v. Garamendi (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 1375, 1392 [insurer that obtained leave to intervene in action "did not become a person against whom a cause of action was asserted simply by filing a complaint in intervention against petitioners. [Insurer] thus could not intervene to make itself a self-styled 'defendant' for purposes of an anti-SLAPP motion when it was not a defendant to the complaint and the complaint did not purport to assert any claim against it."].) Sheng Liu has no standing to file a motion because he is not a party. "It is well settled that only parties with a real interest in a dispute have standing to seek its adjudication. (Code Civ. Proc., § 367.)" (Killian v. Millard (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 1601, 1605.) Indeed, even if the Court agreed with Sheng Liu's arguments, it could not grant any relief or strike any claim because no such claim is alleged against him. The alter ego allegations in the First Amended Cross-Complaint did not add Sheng Liu as a party. Sheng Liu's authorities that provide that an alter ego defendant-i.e. a party-can challenge a pleading or charge of alter ego liability do not give a non-party standing to file a motion. "The alter ego doctrine arises when a plaintiff comes into court claiming that an opposing party is using the corporate form unjustly and in derogation of the plaintiff's interests." (Mesler v. Bragg Management Co. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 290, 300 (emphasis added).) Sheng Liu has no due process right to appear at this point. If Bing Wu ultimately seeks to hold Sheng Liu liable as an alter ego judgment debtor, Bing Wu will need to file a motion and provide Sheng Liu notice. (See NEC Electronics Inc. v. Hurt (1989) 208 Cal.App.3d 772, 778-780.) Moreover, it would only be proper to add Sheng Liu as a debtor if he was in fact controlling this litigation through Lian Tong, LLC. Therefore, Sheng Liu's contention that he needs to be part of this case now to protect his rights rings hollow. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Bing Wu is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Special Motion To Strike Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF LIAN TONG, LLC's Special Motion To Strike Complaint. Cross-defendant Lian Tong, LLC's Anti-SLAPP Motion to Strike the First Amended Cross-Complaint is denied as untimely. Code of Civil Procedure § 425.16(f) provides that "[t]he special motion may be filed within 60 days of the service of the complaint¿." The California Supreme Court has interpreted this provision to mean that "a defendant must move to strike a cause of action within 60 days of service of the earliest complaint that contains that cause of action." (Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2018) 4 Cal.5th 637, 640.) In this case, Bing Wu's original cross-complaint was filed on September 8, 2016. The gravamen of the six count Cross-Complaint is that the parties entered into a 2014 construction contract, the parties subsequently entered into a 2015 release/cancellation agreement that cancelled the 2014 construction agreement, and cross-defendants have breached the 2015 cancellation agreement and/or acted fraudulently by filing the instant action seeking to recover under the 2014 construction contract. The Cross-Complaint alleges: "[a]t all times, cross-defendants never intended to release and or cancel the March 30, 2014 written contract entered into with cross-complainants, and fraudulently induced cross-complainants into entering the February 5, 2015 written contract, to deprive them of $20,500." (Cross-Complaint 14.) The Cross-Complaint further alleges that: "[a]ll cross-defendants breached the contract by not releasing, and not cancelling the March 30, 2014 written contract entered into with cross-complainants since cross-defendants have sued cross-complainants for breach of the March 30, 2014 written contract." (Cross-Complaint 23 (emphasis added).) Bing Wu's amended cross-complaint was filed on January 30, 2019. The First Amended Cross-Complaint alleges the same six causes of action and similarly seeks to recover because cross-defendants failed to release or cancel the March 30, 2014 contract "which is partly exemplified by cross-defendants suing cross-complainants for breach of the March 30, 2014 written contract." (First Amended Cross-Complaint, 32.) The above-cited allegations show that Bing Wu's consistent theory of recovery is that Lian Tong, LLC breached the 2015 release agreement by filing a complaint that seeks to recover on the 2014 construction agreement. The new allegations in the First Amended Cross-Complaint that amplify the allegations regarding Lian Tong, LLC's alleged misrepresentations did not change the nature of the underlying causes of action. Lian Tong, LLC could have attacked the Cross-Complaint based on the petitioning allegations in paragraph 23, but chose not to. Instead, the parties litigated the matter and engaged in extensive discovery over the past two years. Allowing the anti-SLAPP motion at this point would be antithetical to the purpose of the anti-SLAPP statute, which is to quickly and expeditiously dispose of meritless suits. (Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC, 4 Cal.5th at 645.) Therefore, the motion is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Bing Wu is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ROUHEL COTRELL VS. DOUGLAS PARKING LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Change Of Venue To The Superior Court Of Contra Costa County
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT DOUGLAS PARKING LLC's Motion For Change Of Venue To The Superior Court Of Contra Costa County. Defendant Douglas Parking LLC's motion for change of venue to Contra Costa County Superior Court pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 397(c) on the ground of inconvenience of witnesses and promotion of the interests of justice is denied. Defendant has not shown that the interests of justice will be served because of its delay in bringing the motion and the resulting prejudice to plaintiff from a likely further continuance of the trial date. Defendant filed its motion more than a year after it filed its answer, after the parties had already engaged in significant discovery and the trial date had been continued on stipulation of the parties. Although new witnesses were identified during plaintiff's deposition, defendant has not shown that the majority of the witnesses were not already known to it beforehand. Further, significant delay in the setting of the trial date, which involves an incident that occurred more than three years ago, will be prejudicial to plaintiff and will not promote the interests of justice. (See Thompson v. Superior Court (1972) 26 Cal.App.3d 300, 310 [trial court abused its discretion in granting renewed motion for change of venue where moving party failed to act within a reasonable time after knowledge of the alleged change in circumstances, thereby causing prejudice to plaintiffs by causing delay in a prospective trial date].) Defendant's argument in its reply brief that San Francisco is not a proper venue is not properly before the Court. A motion to transfer an improperly venued action must be filed within the time permitted to plead. (Code Civ. Proc. § 396b.) Here, the moving defendant filed its answer in January 2018 without moving to transfer, and therefore forfeited the objection. (See People v. Simon (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1082, 1102 ["any objection that a civil action has been brought in the wrong venue must be raised by a defendant specifically and at the very outset of the proceeding or it will be considered to have been forfeited."]). In any event, the Court declines to entertain a statutory ground for the motion that was not noticed and briefed in the moving papers. (See Regency Outdoor Advertising, Inc. v. Carolina Lanes, Inc. (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1333; McDonald v. California Timber Co. (1907) 151 Cal. 159, 161). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GEODE INVESTMENTS PRIME, LLC ET AL VS. SEAN SHARMA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To Second Cause Of Action For Breach Of Contract Against Defendant Ultratick, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFF GEODE INVESTMENTS BETA, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To Second Cause Of Action For Breach Of Contract Against Defendant Ultratick, Inc. Plaintiff's motion for summary adjudication originally came on for hearing on February 19, 2019. The motion seeks summary adjudication against defendant Ultratick, Inc. and does not seek relief against its manager Sean Sharma. The court tentatively took the motion off calendar because plaintiff filed a notice of settlement on January 14, 2019. Plaintiff and defendant's former counsel appeared at the hearing and explained that Mr. Sharma suddenly passed away before the settlement could be finalized. The court continued the motion to allow the parties to locate a successor in interest who could effectuate the settlement. The court has been informed that Mr. Sharma likely has no next of kin. The court also learned that it is unclear who can act for Ultratick, Inc., which is now acting in pro per based on the substitution of counsel filed on February 1, 2019. The court continues this motion to April 30, 2019, to give Ultratick, Inc. an opportunity to obtain new counsel and determine whether it intends to oppose these motions or finalize the settlement. Plaintiff's counsel shall give notice of the continuance to Ultratick's corporate counsel (Tarak Shah) and former counsel (Andrew Levine). Ultratick will have until April 16, 2019 to file any opposition to this motion if it wishes to do so. If no opposition is filed, the motion will be granted as unopposed on April 30, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JHPDE FINANCE 1, LLC VS. HA T JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF JHPDE FINANCE 1, LLC'S Motion To Deem Rfas Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Greg Groeneveld, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. In the event moving party chooses not appear, the motion will be denied. If the moving party appears, they should address the following issues: 1. The requests for admission were to Ha T. Johnson but served on Bobby Lau as guardian of Ha T. Johnson. The answer in this matter was filed by Ha T. Johnson and purportedly signed by him. There is no mention in the answer of a guardian. Bobby Lau did purportedly sign the proof of service for the answer but there is no mention, in that proof of service or elsewhere that he is the guardian of Mr. Johnson. No authority has been presented that Requests for Admission may be served on a Non-Party. 2. The motion was served on "Bobby Lau guardian of Ha T. Johnson" at the address for Mr. Johnson in San Francisco. No authority has been presented that motion may be served on a Non-Party and, if that was permissible, Mr. Lau's address as stated in the proof of service of the Answer is 5266 Elmer Way, Sacramento, CA 95822. 3. Whether the court should continue this matter to June 14, 2019 (the next scheduled date for this discovery pro tem) with a requirement that the notice of motion be served on Ha T. Johnson and also on Bobby Lau as guardian of Ha T. Johnson at the address in Sacramento or whether the court should deny the motion because the request for admission were improperly served on Bobby Lau as guardian of Ha T. Johnson and the notice of motion was improperly served on Bobby Lau as guardian of Ha T. Johnson. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to greg@greggroeneveldlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ALI OPEYANY VS. LYFT INC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 18, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT LYFT INC's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Lyft Inc.'s motion to strike those portions of plaintiff Ali Opeyany's complaint that seek punitive damages is granted. (See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63 [affirming order striking punitive damages allegations].) In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damages statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) Here, plaintiff's bare allegation, on information and belief, that defendant "willfully and deliberately operated a motor vehicle while on her cell phone" may amount to recklessness but do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her].) Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, relied upon by plaintiff, is distinguishable both because it was decided before the statute was amended in 1987 to require a showing of despicable conduct and on its facts. (Id. at 1212 & n.14.) Plaintiff has not shown how amendment could cure the defect in the complaint. Accordingly, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. If plaintiff develops additional facts in discovery that support a claim for punitive damages, plaintiff may file a motion for leave to amend the complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
